item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is incredibly flattering. I’m glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is, probably, the best executed Keter Duty I've personally read.

item.title: "The first"
item.content:encoded:
I interpreted "the first" as the first iterations of both D class entering the dust and wiping it from their camera lenses. They're buried in information and too large to see, which I take to mean that they have too much depth for the minuscule, flatter iterations to fully comprehend.

The truth dawned on me in exactly the right places, and overall it was a very enjoyable read that established its characters quickly without dragging (well, except for 26…).

Easily a solid +1

item.title: Boy that dialogue was hot
item.content:encoded: There it is the full and only reason this got a plus one from me. It made me believe that mai actually cared about the up-jumped lab rats under their care and, it also made me care about the D-class wich few articles actually bother to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first, uh, the biggest. It's big, enormous, hidden. Buried in information. It'll crush us. Everyone. So we've been made small, simple. Too small to see. Too small to crush.

And the Scarlet King is all I can think about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much appreciate your thoughts, and when I'm writing again, I'm keeping Mai in my back pocket, considering there's been good reception to her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this! I'll try to point out what makes it work for me:

  • The logs are well written and it becomes clear very quickly that their confusing nature is purposeful (as well as not so exaggerated that they become incomprehensible). They also characterize everyone well enough that I'd love to read other stories with Mai or even 26 and 99.
  • The big mystery of what exactly the anomaly does was hinted at enough that I managed to get it at just the right time, near the end of the article, during 99's final speech. I'm not exactly the best at solving these types of mysteries, usually having to rely on either the author or some other user to explain or theorize about the explanation, but this time it was clear enough even for me.
  • The note at the end and the punishment given to Dr. Mai felt like more of a cruelty than detached coldness from Ashby, and from reading the comments here, that seems to have been your intention so I think you succeeded there too (also, 99 saying "you're going to be crushed, Dr. Mai" felt almost like some foreshadowing of this). That being said, I'm a fan of a more human characterization of the Foundation, whether the human aspect in question is positive or negative, so I like seeing this dynamic of a "rookie" Foundation doctor who still sees the D-class as human vs. an older much more cruel Dr. who sees them as mere objects to use in testing. Others who prefer the more cold and distant Foundation might not enjoy this as much.

+1 and hope to see more of Dr. Mai in the future, the character has good potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: edit: Yes, that should be 26. Thanks for the eagle eye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll look forward to that very much, Mai seems ripe for further exploration!

Also quick nitpick:

D-82626: -slippery. We should have brought cleats.

D-82628: Uh.

I think that's a typo, unless I am missing something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very happy you enjoyed it. Mai was a fun character to write; the Foundation is such a cold place in many articles, and I wanted to join the authors who try to explore the warmer and more human side of it. I'm glad that it seemed to come through adequately. I expect Mai will appear in future articles. She's too much of a wildcard to stay put in this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I enjoyed the spooky of the logs, and the confusion of who is actually speaking…

But the thing I really enjoyed was your characterization: I believe Mai’s desire to get them back, even without a bunch of backstory.

Ultimately really effective discussion re: the morality of D-class policies.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might do something here, but I actually have a pitch sitting in the forums right now that is about the origins of the protocol, and will likely have a companion tale depending on how it goes. Then I can link it.

The quick and dirty is that if you are talking to a Foundation agent/D-class/whoever, and you lose confidence of this, then you cut bait. In this article, Edmunson realized that the echoes may actually be coming from an unidentifiable entity or effect of 4372, so he cut contact, because you don’t risk giving SCPs Foundation info, especially if you suspect that they’re mimicking or enthralling personnel to get that info.

I couldn’t think of a classy way to clearly define this without breaking immersion, but I may try to find a way should the tale not get the support I need. I very much appreciate your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adding onto what the others have already said, please expand what the Ashby Protocol is in your article, it seemed like an extremely random move to simply cut contact rather than to attempt to gain more information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree; the Ashby Protocol is likely something I’ll visit again, as is the concept of a PET. I just didn’t want to load it all into one article for the sake of doing so; I already was attacking two different branches of a weirdo SCP and examining the relationship between an ethical researcher and her expendable test subjects. Didn’t want to lay it on too thick for my first run at the site.

I think I’m just going to remove that redacted sentence. I really don’t want anyone discounting the whole article just because of a few black boxes.

Thank you much for the discourse. I’m hoping that people who are inclined to downvote have an opportunity to read through this stuff to find a shortcut to the answer, so I appreciate you giving me the opportunity to lay it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hence, the PET: a team of two D-class who have survived more than a couple of other SCPs and seem to have a rapport with their researcher.

I particularly liked that bit of worldbuilding there. Seems like a fun idea, and I'd love to see it in more articles.

But they don’t even have to deal with the controversy; they can simply claim that if they don’t know for sure, then they might as well keep the theory away from the rank and file to prevent any complaints about just leaving it alone.

I totally understand that, but I think the article would be aided by literal expungement; as in, you only allude to the truth but never explicit mark anything out. If the Ashby Protocol is simple an order of no-contact, then maybe the article could end with a final addendum, reiterating that the Protocol precludes investigation of the anomaly. That'd be rather chilling, I suppose.

I don't want to tell you how to write your article, since you seem like a very competent writer on your own, but it's mostly just an aggregate of small issues here that are leading me to novote. The dialogue, formatting, and clinical tone are all on-point, but I feel like there's a potentiality of this article that's being withheld. The Foundation enforcing a doctrine of ignorance to avoid complications to containment is a chilling violation of ethics and I love every bit of it; I just wish it were examined more closely by the article, I suppose.

Well, anyways, best of luck! I can tell you've got a pretty bright future on the site, if you can craft this on your first attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I imagine the D-class are the same for me as anyone else; they’re expendable test subjects. But they’re still people. I didn’t want to use an MTF or agent to explore an unknown SCP with no clear threat, but I did want someone who could operate a bit more competently. Hence, the PET: a team of two D-class who have survived more than a couple of other SCPs and seem to have a rapport with their researcher. I wanted to show what happens when the Foundation fails to properly indoctrinate their doctors into treating humans inhumanely. It’s not so much a war as it is simply a doctor who ended up caring for a couple of her test subjects.

2) The redactions seem to be contentious. The reason is that if they acknowledge the possibility that there are living people inside, that might obligate them in some way. As it stands, they can’t confirm that anyone is actually alive in there, or that there aren’t hostiles. A rescue mission would be ill-advised. But they don’t even have to deal with the controversy; they can simply claim that if they don’t know for sure, then they might as well keep the theory away from the rank and file to prevent any complaints about just leaving it alone.

Hope this clarifies. I’m really wrestling with the redactions if that’s what’s turning people off to this, but after I had a couple of people in chat guess the right answer for what was happening, I really wanted to keep this a little bit of a justified puzzle.

Thanks for asking, I appreciate it.

item.title: Re: Promising, but negative for now
item.content:encoded:
1) I think that they’re happier saying, “Those people are gone, there are no Foundation personnel inside the SCP” than they are admitting that there might be living people who could conceivably be rescued. They don’t exactly know what happens in there per se, but they aren’t interested in debating whether they should attempt to extract them, which is exactly what Mai tried to do.

2) From the Foundation’s view, this wouldn’t be cruel. She has to learn to deal with this to succeed in the Foundation; they can’t simply have her as an ethical live wire. They have justified reasons for using expendable people in tests and maintenance. Mai comes from “the real world” where this would be absurd, but the Foundation doesn’t work by regular ethics. They need her to be comfortable sending people to their deaths, and perhaps forcing her to do it over and over would sharpen her into the researcher they want. It’s absurd to us, but it would be fairly logical for them, even if we might disagree whether it’s effective or not.

3) This is largely the reason why I didn’t make a direct 001 connection. It’s simply implied that it’s something big and scary, or at least 99 believes so. Exactly what that is might be the subject of an established or new SCP, but this article focuses on the “safe room” that seems to exist to counter it.

4) I didn’t mean to imply that the Foundation has any desire to use this SCP. They don’t know exactly what it is. They know that 99, or something claiming to be 99, thinks it’s a safe place, but they’re not going to march anyone else in there.

Hope this clears a few things up. I appreciate you working through your doubts with me. I’m getting the impression that others might be struggling to piece the puzzle together as well. I wrote this for people like me who love these SCPs that make you work a bit for the answer.

Edit: I removed the redacted sentence about the byproduct. It’s causing more problems than it’s worth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a perfectly serviceable article, such that I feel comfortable novoting, but I have two concerns in particular.

Firstly, what are D-Class in your personal headcanon? The Ds in this article are so professional and amicable with Foundation personnel that they seem more like task force operatives. Having the Foundation not be essentially at war with their workforce is a valid headcanon, but this portrayal deviates so far from the norm that I feel it needs explicit clarification.

Secondly, the redactions. The Foundation covering something up for ethical reasons is also valid, although it does seem to contradict the whole "amicable D-Class" idea you have elsewhere. But I don't think they'd cover up the truth about an anomaly; ethical redactions would be saved for when the Foundation itself has done something terrible. Maybe that borders on personal headcanon, but when you deal in existential threats, I doubt the revelation that the dust is people would be particularly disconcerting, and not something they'd want to specifically redact. If anything, I think the Foundation knowing that we are the dust and not really caring is a greater expression of horror and worldbuilding than what's right here.

item.title: Re: Promising, but negative for now
item.content:encoded:
1) While I'm still not sold on the redaction, I understand it a bit more. The idea that the people are the dust makes this darker and more interesting too. I don't see how people knowing about what the scp does would complicate the containment, they are not doing this the anomaly is, and ethically I don't see any reason why they should risk people trying to fix the anomaly unless they plan to use it. I don't think it would compromise the containment at the very least.

I imagine there to be a way to reverse the dust back to people then if this is supposed to be a safe room? Or maybe secret research into turning it into a safe room?

2) I can agree with the fact that the Foundation would not want to inhibit Mai's ability to work, but by sticking her in a MORE traumatic place I would think that it would increase the likelihood of her being unable to perform. Rather than taking that risk I think they would move her to a quieter place, perhaps try again later. And the fact that you state that "It would be a nightmare for Mai" does not fit with my idea of the Foundation. I like that tagline, "Cold but not Cruel". There is no purpose in needless suffering, and the Foundation is supposed to prevent needless suffering. That whole, "we die in the dark so you can live in the light" thing. Ultimately I don't think they would risk wasting a promising researcher.

3) It doesn't really seem like a "Safe place" to hide from whatever 001 proposal you are referring to, but it is an anomaly and so it's weird. While I know I don't connect dots as well as other people, I don't think there is really enough here to indicate the connection to 001. My first thought was that it was an indication of some out-of-reality being, perhaps a malevolent god or something like scp 3125. Which I guess is actually along the lines of what you were hoping for, so maybe I just didn't connect the last step.

4) Alright, that's reasonable. Personally, I could see the Foundation doing a little bit more testing through "Interviews" to extract more data, but given the idea behind it I guess it would go against the purpose.

I don't think I'm going to upvote this right now, honestly I'm not really a fan of the Foundation stopping testing like they did because they want to use this as a secret safe room. But I will remove my downvote, I can see you put a lot of effort into this and it does come together a bit more now.

item.title: Re: Promising, but negative for now
item.content:encoded:
I very much appreciate the feedback. You had good points, allow me to make a few brief responses.

1) I do know what's behind the black boxes, and the logs hint at what it is as well. The "big secret" here is that as individuals (and their instances) become smaller and more compact (so very small, so very close) as they approach the center, they would appear from the outside to be a light. Just as well, those tiny, tiny, tiny instances would then become the dust itself, meaning they were wiping people from their camera lenses. I was inspired by 2719 to write a "mystery SCP" that had a definite answer, but required a bit of work to figure it out. A couple of reviewers were able to do so, which is why I felt confident enough to post it to the site. It would be redacted by the Foundation because the ethics of that whole situation would likely complicate the relatively easy containment they're managing already.

2) That letter has been more divisive than anything. The original version had an extra paragraph that spelled this out a bit, but the Foundation moving Mai to an SCP that "eats" D-class seems to be a very Foundationy move to me. They don't want to kill Mai, or inhibit her ability to work, so instead, they stick her with an SCP that would force her to treat D-class expendably. Would she put up a fight? Maybe, but on the other hand, this would be pretty classic conditioning via immersion therapy, which is right up the Foundation's alley. It would be a nightmare for Mai.

3) "The first" is my coy reference that this SCP is a safe room from 001, whatever that might be. I didn't want to directly cross-link to 001 on my first article, as I didn't want to piggyback success, but that's the idea behind it. It's also why the Foundation would not be crazy about publicly investigating this much further, since they likely have a good idea of what that danger is already.

4) The Ashby Protocol is something I made up after being offered no existing solutions. Basically, it's the idea that if you no longer can be certain you're talking to Foundation personnel, you cut bait. That seems to be in line with what a paranoid, secret organization would do. I'm open to suggestions, but I tried to imply the definition without being lame and outright stating it.

I very much appreciate your feedback. I'm thinking I might just need to give away what the "big secret" of this thing is so people understand that yes, there is an answer to the questions I've asked here.

Also, I appreciate the edit.

Thank you much.

item.title: Promising, but negative for now
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so I'm writing this as I read to keep track of thoughts.

  • First off, why is the fact that the light is the byproduct of something redacted? Right now without more info it just seems like you couldn't think of something to put there.
  • While I like the fact that the d class and the staff are talking like normal people, one gripe is that they record the fact that Mai stifled a laugh. Doesn't seem like something relevant, especially with the note right above it about removing irrelevant communication. It does however change the attitude for the next line where Mai scolds the D-Class into something less harsh, so I can understand the inclusion.
+ show block
– hide block
  • I like the fact that we can't rely on the transcripts, while not a new idea it typically interests me. Especially when it makes me question which D-26 or D-99 is the real one, did he become effected by the light and is the one muttering strange things or is he the one begging to be returned? Are either of them original?
  • Alright, at the end. I really dislike the movement of Dr. Mai to the new SCP. While I completely expected her to be disciplined for her behavior, I can't believe that after showing that she couldn't handle this job where she lost 2 D-class, she would be put into an apparently dangerous position in charge of MORE D-Class. It's like immersion therapy but it could actually harm the patient AND other people. Not to mention is seems cruel rather than cold. The foundation has tons of positions, and typically when I read someone being moved because of a similar situation they are moved to a place where they don't have as much chance of fucking things up. Pushing this woman like they seem to do will likely not make her "gain a deeper understanding of how the Foundation utilizes our D-Class personnel" (What?!), but make her less stable, possibly turning her into a security risk. Give her a damn desk job if you have to, move her to a quiet scp, hell stick her with 999 for a week and some therapy for awhile and reevaluate her personality.

Anyways, while there isn't anything particularly special about this scp, there doesn't need to be. I like the premise, I like the way you go about things sometimes, but overall I end up a bit on the negative side. I think you can make this into an article that I would upvote however, and I don't want you to think I hated it. I actually enjoyed reading it except for the last part.

The twist of "Hiding from the first, the biggest" would also probably be investigated a bit more by the Foundation. Even just a note saying something along the lines of "No information found to verify claims" or "subsequent communication not found to be coherent or reliable" should probably be added. I doubt that the Foundation would just ignore the ability to speak to the things in this anomaly even if the words can't really be trusted. More information is always a good thing.

Also, unless those things would come out of the anomaly, I would probably designate it as safe. Though there are sapient (maybe?) beings in it, it doesn't appear they will ever leave, but I can understand Euclid.

Lastly, I don't recognize Ashby Protocol, and looking it up through google got me nothing. Did they terminate all communication with the anomaly? Why? It appeared that nobody outside the anomaly was in danger. Unless Mai was under the affect of a cognitohazard, in which case it should be stated.

I know this was a wall of mostly negative text, sorry about that. But take it as you grabbing my interest enough for me to care this much. Also, feel free to point out any errors in my understanding.

Edit: Perhaps a note that the threats heard about the *Biggest one* are thought to be simply another form of lure to make people enter the light? I also read the greenlight thread, and understand a bit more now. However I would double down on the fact that the cause of the light in the center should not be redacted. While it does kind of give away what will happen to the D-Class, if this were an actual report you would not redact the cause of the light to build tension. Perhaps make it vague, such as the light is a byproduct of the multitude of compressed objects approaching the center.

Edit Edit: I noticed in the page at the bottom you addressed the message to "To: Dr. Dr. Mai Giang Lê". I removed the second Dr. for you.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: So safe.
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad to see this posted. I really enjoyed the narrative here, and the dialogue between the Foundation staff and the D-class really gave the impression of real people. I know that most of the Foundation staff have become desensitized to the often gruesome ends the D-class come to, but I'm sure some of them still see the D-class as human. It is important to remember that even though most of the D-class are reprobates, they're all still human and we should recognize that humanity.

To fail to do so would be to risk losing our own.

item.title: Acknowledgements
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to the following users for their feedback and sharpening of this story:

PrLosash
cybersqyd
MalyceGraves (BIG THANK YOU for formatting help!)

Greenlight thread

Added a dinky little image I whipped up using basic paint effects until I can land something better.

Edit: I’ve had quite a few rates without the needle moving much, so I suppose there’s some division here. While it’s obviously not obligatory, I welcome any and all comments, including from those who don’t like it. Since I’m a greenlighter, it’s important for me to have an idea of what isn’t working in my own article.

Edit 2: I didn't want to do this initially, but I think some people are downvoting because the big answer behind this that makes things click isn't explicitly stated, and while a couple of people have gotten it, I'm getting the impression that many people are not. So here's what's behind the redactions and such:

The Big Secret
Individuals (and their instances) become smaller and more compact (so very small, so very close) as they approach the center, they would appear from the outside to be a light. Just as well, those tiny, tiny, tiny instances would then become the dust itself, meaning they were wiping people from their camera lenses (hence the biological makeup of the dust). It would be redacted by the Foundation because the ethics of that whole situation would likely complicate the relatively easy containment they're managing already.

I was inspired by 2719 to write a "mystery SCP" that had a definite answer, but required a bit of work to figure it out. Maybe it's too much. Either way, if this answer helps makes everything make more sense, I hope it might be reevaluated with the knowledge that I wasn't using the black boxes to escape questions I didn't know the answer to.

Edit 2: Removed some redactions that seemed to be more trouble that they were worth.
